text: New technologies promise to revolutionise humanitarian action and, in the process, raise all sorts of difficult ethical and operational questions. Little of this debate is being carried out in the formal discourse, and is instead being left to evolve in an unregulated space of blogs, microblogs and listservs. Rather than simply trying to highlight the risks, this article argues for the need for a new generation of scholarly research into humanitarian technology and its implications for human suffering in both conflict and disaster scenarios. Drawing both on ethnographic research on aid work in contexts of war and destruction, and on theories of governance, bureaucracy and technology, the paper seeks to explore and understand the dynamics of humanitarian action and its increasingly mediated forms, and to unpack some of the structural dynamics of the technology of aid work itself, so that it can be more consciously considered.
